full_response:
In special relativity, a vector (specifically a contravariant four-vector) is defined by its transformation properties under Lorentz transformations: \( V'^\mu = \Lambda^\mu_{\ \nu} V^\nu \), where \(\Lambda\) is the Lorentz transformation matrix. The partial derivative operator \(\partial_a \equiv \frac{\partial}{\partial x^a}\) has a lower index and transforms under a change of coordinates \(x \to x'\) as:

\[
\frac{\partial}{\partial x'^\mu} = \frac{\partial x^\nu}{\partial x'^\mu} \frac{\partial}{\partial x^\nu}.
\]

For Lorentz transformations, \(x'^\mu = \Lambda^\mu_{\ \nu} x^\nu\), the Jacobian matrix is \(\frac{\partial x'^\mu}{\partial x^\nu} = \Lambda^\mu_{\ \nu}\), so \(\frac{\partial x^\nu}{\partial x'^\mu} = (\Lambda^{-1})^\nu_{\ \mu}\). Thus,

\[
\partial'_\mu = (\Lambda^{-1})^\nu_{\ \mu} \partial_\nu,
\]

which is the transformation law for a covariant vector (one-form), not a contravariant vector. Therefore, \(\partial_a\) is not a (contravariant) four-vector in special relativity; it is a one-form.

\boxed{\partial_a \text{ transforms as a covariant vector (one-form), not as a contravariant vector under Lorentz transformations.}}

Confidence: 95%
extracted_answer: \partial_a \text{ transforms as a covariant vector (one-form), not as a contravariant vector under Lorentz transformations.}
